text: Good evening. Let’s take a few minutes to reflect on your day — not to judge it, but to understand it.
How would you describe your day overall?
When things went well today, what made the biggest difference?
When things didn’t go as planned, what was your first reaction?
options: I looked for what I could control
I waited for things to improve
I focused on what went wrong

text: You’re noticing your role in what worked. That awareness of your own actions is what builds real control over time.
Difficult days often pull attention toward what’s outside our control. Still, there are usually small choices we make within them — noticing those is where change begins.
When you faced a challenge today, how did you respond?
options: I took initiative to move forward
I asked for help when needed

text: You stayed engaged with the situation instead of stepping away from it. That willingness to act is a strong foundation for growth.
Stepping back or delaying can feel easier in the moment. Recognizing that pattern is the first step toward changing it.
You’ve reflected on how you handled situations. Now let’s shift to what you contributed during the day.
Which of these best describes a moment from your day?
options: I helped someone beyond my responsibility
I did what was expected of me
I felt others should have done more

text: You leaned toward contributing. Even small actions like helping or taking initiative build stronger teams over time.
It’s natural to notice what we needed or expected. Shifting even a little toward what we can give often changes how the day feels.
What was your main focus throughout the day?
Where you place your focus shapes how your work feels. When effort connects to purpose or contribution, it often becomes more meaningful.
Now let’s widen the lens a bit — beyond just your tasks.
When you think about your day, who comes to mind most?
Focusing only on yourself is natural, especially on busy days. Expanding that view — even slightly — can change how situations feel.
You’re already considering people beyond yourself. That wider perspective often brings more clarity and meaning.
Looking back, how did your actions affect others today?
Every action creates ripple effects, whether we notice them or not. Awareness of that impact is what helps shape better choices over time.
Today you explored how you responded to situations, what you contributed, and how you related to others. Small shifts in any of these areas can gradually change how your days unfold.
That’s it for today. See you tomorrow.
